narrative.prPianoRule: Right hand: melody + upper harmony (register C4–C6). Left hand: bass + accompaniment (register A0–C4). Sustain pedal: prolongs and connects harmonies. Avoid too many notes in the low register (muddy sound) and too many in the high register (shrill).
narrative.prPianoExample: Chopin Nocturne: lyrical melody in the right hand (register E4–G5), wide arpeggios in the left hand (register C2–G3), sustain pedal held throughout the bar.
narrative.exRavelDesc: The same snare-drum rhythmic ostinato, the same melody in C major, the same harmony — repeated 18 times with progressively denser orchestration. Each repetition adds instruments or changes combinations. A real-time study in orchestration.
narrative.exDebussyName: Debussy — Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune (1894)
narrative.exDebussyDesc: Orchestral impressionism at its finest: solo flute, harp, muted strings, stopped horns, woodwinds in half-tints. Each instrument in its most poetic register. No loud brass — everything is suggestion and colour.
narrative.exBeethovenDesc: A model of economy of means: a 4-note motif distributed across all families. The orchestration follows the intensity of the discourse — strings + woodwinds at p, tutti fortissimo at key moments. Every instrument in its rightful place.

cours18.title: Motivic Development
cours18.subtitle: The 5 elements of a motif, the repetition paradox, and the 4 families of techniques — from harmony (least radical) to rhythm (most radical).

quiz.perfect: Excellent — motivic development holds no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more techniques to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep listening to the transformations — the ear learns with practice!

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: In 1808, Beethoven premiered his 5th Symphony. The opening motif — G–G–G–E♭, just four notes — generates twenty minutes of music. A student asked how he could build so much from so little. Beethoven replied: “That is exactly the right question. Richness lies not in the quantity of material — it lies in the depth with which you develop it.”
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Before composing, ask yourself: is my opening motif short enough to be memorable? Distinctive enough to be recognisable? Flexible enough to be transformed? A motif that answers yes to all three can generate a symphony. One that is too complex is hard to develop; too simple, it exhausts itself quickly.
narrative.motifP1: The motif is the smallest distinct and immediately recognisable musical unit. It serves as the guiding thread of musical discourse: repeated and transformed, it gives coherence and direction to a phrase, a movement, or an entire work. Unlike support music (which accompanies an external action), discourse music develops its own ideas autonomously.
narrative.motifDemoTitle: The most famous motif: Beethoven's 5th Symphony
narrative.motifDemoDesc: Just 4 notes — 3 repeated + a descending minor third. This motif is fragmented, transposed, augmented and liquidated across all 4 movements of the symphony: 20 minutes of music from a 4-note cell.
narrative.motifH3Elements: The 5 constituent elements of a motif
narrative.elem1Desc: The characteristic melodic leaps — the distinctive melodic identity (third, fifth, tritone). Beethoven's motif: a descending minor third G→E♭.
narrative.elem2Desc: The distinctive rhythmic pattern — often more identifiable than the melody. Three short notes + one long (ta-ta-ta-TAA) in Beethoven.
narrative.elem3Desc: The chords that support and colour the motif — determining whether the expression feels tense, resolved, or ambiguous.
narrative.elem4Desc: The texture surrounding the motif: arpeggios, block chords, independent bass line, counterpoint.
narrative.elem5Desc: Nuance, articulation, attack — the physical expressive character. Forte or piano, legato or staccato.
narrative.motifWarnBox: The paradox of repetition: repetition is necessary — without it, the listener recognises nothing and the musical discourse loses all coherence. But it is dangerous if too pure: repeating identically more than twice quickly breeds monotony. The solution is to repeat while transforming — maintain recognition while renewing interest.
narrative.motifH3Rhetorique: The motif as character: 3 rhetorical stages
narrative.step1Desc: Establish the motif's identity — present it clearly and unambiguously.
narrative.step2Desc: Anchor the motif in the listener's ear — repeat (transformed) to confirm the identity.
narrative.step3Desc: Transform, develop, combine — the motif travels and matures.
narrative.ex1Desc: Just four notes. Beethoven fragments, transposes, augments and liquidates this motif throughout all four movements. Twenty minutes of symphony from a four-note cell.
narrative.ex2Motif: Motif associated with a character or idea
narrative.ex2Desc: Wagner assigns a motif to each character (Siegfried's sword, the curse of the Gold). The motif returns transformed according to the dramatic state — a motif of glory becomes sinister through harmonic change.
narrative.ex3Desc: Williams inherits directly from Wagner: each character or concept has its leitmotif. The Force theme is transformed into a military march for Darth Vader — same contour, radically different harmonic context.
narrative.motifInfoBox: The motif is not an ornament — it is the seed of all musical thought. Before composing a phrase, ask yourself: is my core idea distinctive enough to be recognisable? Flexible enough to be transformed in 10 different ways? A good idea generates a phrase; an excellent idea generates a symphony.
narrative.techP1: Development techniques are organised into 4 families, ranked from least to most radical. We begin by transforming the context (harmony, dynamics) before touching the melody, and we modify rhythm last — as it is often the most identifying element of the motif.
narrative.famAOrder: First — the least radical changes. The melodic and rhythmic identity of the motif remains intact.
narrative.famADesc: Transforming the harmonic context or the accompanying texture. The motif remains fully recognisable because its melody and rhythm are preserved — only the 'colour' changes.
narrative.famASub1Detail: Replace I with VI (diatonic substitution), or V7 with its tritone substitute. Same melody, different harmonic colour.
narrative.famASub2Detail: Transform the bass into independent counterpoint — parallel thirds or sixths, or contrary motion to the melody.
narrative.famASub3Detail: Break chords into successive ascending or descending notes. Transforms the texture without touching the melodic motif.
narrative.famASub4Detail: Add an independent voice that answers or imitates the motif. Enriches polyphonic density.
narrative.famBOrder: Second — expressive changes without altering notes or rhythm.
narrative.famBDesc: Modifying expressive parameters: dynamics, articulation, register, timbre. The motif keeps its structure intact — only its 'energy' or 'costume' changes.
narrative.famBSub1Detail: Forte → piano, crescendo, decrescendo. Same melody, radically different dramatic intensity contrast.
narrative.famBSub2Detail: Legato → staccato, accents on unexpected beats. Changes the character without touching pitches or durations.
narrative.famBSub3Detail: Transpose by an octave: going up brings brightness and urgency; going down creates gravity and depth.
narrative.famBSub4Detail: Change the instrument or register: same motif in the strings, then brass, then tutti — entirely different effects.
narrative.famCOrder: Third — the pitches change, but the rhythm often remains recognisable.
narrative.famCDesc: Modifying the melodic contour of the motif while often maintaining its rhythmic structure. The ear recognises the motif by its rhythm even when pitches change significantly.
narrative.famCSub1Detail: Each interval is inverted: an ascending third becomes a descending third. G–G–G–E♭ becomes G–G–G–B♭.
narrative.famCSub2Detail: The motif is played backwards — from the last note to the first. E♭–G–G–G (retrograde of Beethoven's motif).
narrative.famCSub3Detail: Insert passing notes, or remove a note from the motif. The contour remains recognisable, lightened or enriched.
narrative.famCSub4Detail: Pitches shift according to the scale (harmonic sequence) but the rhythmic pattern remains identical.
narrative.famDOrder: Last — the most radical changes. The motif may become unrecognisable.
narrative.famDDesc: Modifying note durations or metric structure. Rhythmic alterations are the most transformative: rhythm is often the most distinctive feature of a motif — to touch it is to risk it.
narrative.famDSub1Detail: Double (or triple) the duration of each note. The motif slows down, takes on a solemn grandeur — Beethoven uses this in sonata developments.
narrative.famDSub2Detail: Divide the duration of each note. The motif speeds up, becomes nervous or urgent — liquidation effect at the end of a phrase.
narrative.famDSub3Detail: Moving from 4/4 to 3/4 (or vice versa). The same motif in a different metre creates a feeling of imbalance or lightness.
narrative.famDSub4Detail: Transform syncopations into strong beats (regularisation) or strong beats into syncopations (deregulation). Changes rhythmic energy without touching pitches.
narrative.techArpegeFormula: C – E – G – C  (ascending broken chord)
narrative.techArpegeDesc: Accompanying with arpeggios transforms the texture without touching the melodic motif.
narrative.techContrepointDesc: Two voices in contrary motion — the bass ascends while the upper voice descends.
narrative.techTipBox: Combinations: the 4 families combine freely. A motif can simultaneously receive a harmonic substitution (A), be played pianissimo (B), and be slightly transposed (C). Combinations create the richest transformations — this is exactly what Beethoven does in his great developments.

cours17.title: Musical Phrase and Form
cours17.subtitle: Motif, 4-step development, repetition techniques, antecedent-consequent period and major musical forms.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — musical phrase and form hold no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few forms still to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep listening to the examples — the ear learns to recognise forms with practice!

narrative.phraseIntro: The musical phrase is the basic unit of musical discourse. It rests on a motif — the smallest distinctive musical element — which is repeated and transformed in 4 typical steps. Unlike support music (which accompanies an action), discourse music develops its own ideas autonomously.
narrative.beethovenBoxDesc: Just 4 notes — and yet the seed of an entire symphony. This motif is fragmented, transposed, augmented, and liquidated throughout all 4 movements.
narrative.stepsTitle: The 4 steps of a phrase (8 bars)
narrative.step1Desc: Presentation of the main motif — the statement of the musical subject.
narrative.step2Desc: Strict or transformed — anchors the motif in the listener's ear.
narrative.step3Desc: Fragmentation and liquidation — the harmonic rhythm accelerates.
narrative.step4Desc: Conclusive harmonic resolution (V for half-cadence, I for perfect cadence).
narrative.liquidationBox: Liquidation (Schoenberg's term): at the end of a phrase (step 3), the motif progressively loses its distinctive characteristics. First the rhythm is retained, then just the contour, then almost nothing — the phrase dissolves into the cadence. A highly expressive rhetorical device for 'conclusion'.
narrative.techStricteDesc: The motif is repeated identically — same pitches, same rhythm. The most direct technique: the listener recognizes the motif unambiguously. Used only once before varying.
narrative.techTransfoDesc: The motif is transposed to another pitch while retaining its rhythmic contour. The rhythmic identity maintains coherence; the new pitch brings variety.
narrative.techMarcheDesc: The motif repeats at successive scale degrees (tonal sequence) or by strict transposition (real sequence). Schoenberg carefully distinguishes the two types: the tonal sequence stays diatonic, the real sequence may leave the scale.
narrative.techComplementaireDesc: A second motif answers the first and completes the rhythmic or melodic space. Together, the two motifs form the complete phrase — each is incomplete without the other.
narrative.techHarmoniqueDesc: The melodic contour is preserved but the chords underneath change, altering the harmonic colour. The melody is recognizable; the harmony renews the expression.
narrative.warnBox: Key point: repetition must be varied — repeating identically more than twice creates monotony. The art is to maintain recognition of the motif (coherence) while renewing it (variety). Beethoven never repeats the same motif in exactly the same way twice in a row.
narrative.periodeTitle: The period and the large forms
narrative.periodeIntro: The period is a structure of two phrases: the antecedent asks a question (weak cadence on V), the consequent provides the answer (strong cadence on I). The large forms organize these periods into contrasting sections to build a musical discourse on a larger scale.
narrative.antecedentDesc: The half-cadence on V leaves the phrase 'open' — the ear awaits a resolution.
narrative.consequentDesc: The perfect cadence on I resolves the tension — the 'answer' is given, the period is complete.
narrative.periodCompleteBtn: ▶ Listen to the complete period (antecedent + consequent)
narrative.formeAbaDesc: Section A (main), contrasting section B (new key or mode), return of A. The symmetry creates balance and satisfaction: we depart, we return.
narrative.formeAbaExamples: Chopin Nocturnes, opera arias (aria da capo)
narrative.formeAABADesc: Section A (8 bars) repeated twice, contrasting bridge B (8 bars), return of A (8 bars). Standard form of American jazz from the 1930s–60s. The bridge B is often in a neighbouring key.
narrative.formeAABAExamples: Autumn Leaves, I Got Rhythm, Stardust, Over the Rainbow
narrative.formeBluesDesc: Question/answer/resolution in 12 bars: tonic statement (4 bars), subdominant response (2 bars), return (2 bars), turnaround (4 bars). Predictable structure exploited by musicians and improvisers.
narrative.formeBluesExamples: 12-bar blues, jazz blues with II–V substitutions
narrative.formeABACDesc: Variant of the standard form: A returns twice but ends differently (B then C). Each return of A creates a slight surprise in the conclusion.
narrative.formeABACExamples: All The Things You Are, My Romance
narrative.formeSonateDesc: Exposition: theme A (tonic) + theme B (dominant/relative). Development: thematic manipulation in distant keys. Recapitulation: A and B in tonic. The most complex form of classicism.
narrative.formeSonateExamples: 1st movement of sonatas by Beethoven, Mozart, Haydn
narrative.tipBox: Jazz and form: an improviser who knows the form plays 'with' the structure. More tension in bridge B, more marked resolution at the return of A, climax at the right moment of the chorus. Ignoring the form means improvising in a vacuum — technically correct, musically inexpressive. Form is the framework of discourse.
narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: At UCLA in the 1940s, Schoenberg asked his students to analyze Beethoven's 5th Symphony: 'See how these four notes — Sol–Sol–Sol–Mib — generate twenty minutes of symphony. A motif is not an ornament: it is the seed of all musical thought.' His treatise Fundamentals of Musical Composition formalises these principles for his American classes.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: The motif is the smallest unit of musical discourse. Before composing a phrase, ask yourself: is my basic idea distinctive enough to be recognizable? Flexible enough to be transformed? A good idea generates a phrase; an excellent idea generates a symphony.
narrative.beethovenFormulaCaption: Sol – Sol – Sol – Mib  (3 repetitions + minor third drop)

cours11.title: Chord Extensions
cours11.subtitle: 9ths, 11ths, 13ths — stacking thirds beyond the 7th to enrich harmonic color.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — chord extensions hold no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more chords to practice and you'll have it.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep listening actively — the ear learns with practice!

narrative.extIntro: A basic chord (triad or tetrad) can be enriched by extensions: we continue stacking thirds beyond the 7th. The 9th is the 2nd an octave higher, the 11th is the 4th an octave higher, the 13th is the 6th an octave higher.
narrative.extInfoBox: The chain of thirds: root → 3rd → 5th → 7th → 9th → 11th → 13th. Each step adds a third. A complete 13th chord contains 7 notes — the entire scale at once. In practice, several are omitted to maintain clarity.
narrative.extClickHint: Click an extension to hear it and see its details.
narrative.maitreAnecdote: Bill Evans had studied Ravel, Debussy, and Chopin before joining Miles Davis in 1958. When Kind of Blue was recorded, Evans introduced a radically new voicing style: 9ths in inner voices, 11ths in clusters, 13ths replacing 5ths. His trio (with Scott LaFaro and Paul Motian) redefined chamber jazz. Waltz for Debby (1961) remains the benchmark for piano extensions.
narrative.maitreLesson: A 9th doesn't 'replace' a chord tone — it enriches it. The difference between a chord and a color is often just one note an octave higher.
narrative.appIntro: Extensions apply to all three tonal functions (T, SD, D) without changing their role in the progression. Dm9 remains subdominant, G13 remains dominant, CMaj9 remains tonic — but each chord gains richness and color.
narrative.appTensionsInfoBox: The available tensions rule: an extension is « available » if it does not create a ♭9 interval (semitone + octave) with a chord tone. The ♭9 is the unacceptable dissonance — except when it is itself the desired tension.
narrative.appWarnBox: Key exception: the perfect 11th is avoided on major chords (it creates a ♭9 with the major 3rd), but it is available and very common on minor chords (the ♭3 is a whole tone away from the 11th). That is why Dm11 works, but CMaj11 sounds wrong.
narrative.appTipBox: Practical voicing: in jazz piano, keep 3rd + 7th as the core (shell voicing) and add the desired tension in the soprano or an inner voice. The root is played by the bass — no need to double it at the piano.
narrative.quizFeedbackExcellent: Excellent — chord extensions hold no more secrets!
narrative.quizFeedbackGood: Good — a few more chords to practice and you'll be perfect.
narrative.quizFeedbackKeepGoing: Keep listening to extensions actively — the ear learns!

cours22.title: Reharmonization
cours22.subtitle: Transform a progression while preserving the melody — diatonic and tritone substitutions, modal borrowing, parallel harmonization.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — reharmonization holds no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more progressions to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep listening to the progressions — the ear learns by doing!

narrative.principeIntro: Reharmonising means replacing the chords of a progression while keeping the melody intact. The fundamental constraint: every melody note must remain consonant with the new chord.
narrative.principeInfoBox: Principle: the melody is fixed — it is "the roof". The chords can change — they are "the floor". Choosing a new chord means choosing the light under which the melody will be heard.
narrative.principeClickHint: Click on a technique to see and hear the progressions.
narrative.principeWarnBox: Melodic check: before any reharmonisation, identify the long notes of the melody. A held note that creates a half-step with a chord tone = strong dissonance. Check systematically.
narrative.applicationIntro: Three examples from the jazz repertoire, each illustrating a different technique. Compare the original and reharmonised progressions by listening to them.
narrative.applicationTip: Exercise: take a standard you know (Autumn Leaves, All The Things You Are) and reharmonise 2–3 chords using the techniques from this lesson. Start with diatonic substitution — it is the closest to the original.
narrative.quizFeedbackExcellent: Excellent — reharmonisation holds no more secrets!
narrative.quizFeedbackGood: Good — just a few more progressions to listen to and it will be perfect.
narrative.quizFeedbackKeepGoing: Keep listening to progressions actively — the ear learns!
narrative.maitreAnecdote: In 1958, Bill Evans recorded 'Peace Piece' in a single take. For eight minutes, he explored every harmonic colour possible over a single bass pedal — C. He borrowed chords from the minor mode, slid chromatic Maj7 blocks down, never leaving the root C. This piece is a manifesto of reharmonisation as colour.
narrative.maitreLesson: Reharmonising means seeing the same notes under different lights. The melody is the soloist — the chords are the light you choose to illuminate it.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Brahms spent years studying Bach and Beethoven before publishing his first symphony at age 43. He said he could hear the footsteps of giants behind him. Yet his style is immediately recognizable: dense harmonies, syncopated rhythms, Nordic melancholy. Studying the masters does not dilute one's style — it forges it.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Knowing the styles of others is the shortest path to finding your own.
narrative.stylesIntro: Every composer or movement has a harmonic signature — a set of techniques, progressions and colors that make it immediately recognizable. Click on a style to hear its typical progression.
narrative.stylesInfoBox: How to recognize a style? Look for these 3 clues: (1) which chords are used (diatonic? borrowed? extensions?); (2) how the chords follow each other (by fifths? by thirds? in parallel?); (3) is there a V7→I resolution or not?
narrative.stylesClickHint: Click on a style to see its characteristics and hear its typical progression.
narrative.stylesWarnBox: Styles are not rigid boxes: a composer can blend several styles in a single piece. Chopin borrows from Bach (counterpoint) and anticipates Debussy (modal borrowings). Recognizing a style means identifying the dominant language, not making an absolute classification.
narrative.exIntro: For each style, choose the progression that matches it. Listen to each option before answering — the ear guides theory.
narrative.exTipBox: Method: listen to all options first (▶ button) before choosing. The ear recognizes the style before the theory — trust your listening, then check with the explanations.

cours37.title: Advanced Analysis: Schenker and Motivic Analysis
cours37.subtitle: Ursatz, Urlinie, 4 reduction levels and motivic analysis — seeing the deep structure of a work beyond its surface.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered Schenkerian analysis and motivic analysis.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! Review the 4 reduction levels and motivic transformations.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep going — try again when you're ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Schoenberg is famous for twelve-tone music, but he was also a major analyst and theorist. He developed the concept of Grundgestalt (basic shape) — a 2-to-6-note motif from which an entire work unfolds. His manual 'Fundamentals of Musical Composition' is still used in conservatories. Paradox: the father of atonality was obsessed with the motivic coherence that only tonal music can establish clearly.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Analysis does not explain why music is beautiful — it reveals the framework beneath the surface. It is a tool, not a value.
narrative.schenkerP: Heinrich Schenker (1868–1935) developed a theory according to which all tonal music is an elaboration of a simple fundamental structure — the Ursatz. His approach reveals the layers of meaning hidden beneath the surface of music.
narrative.schenkerInfoBox: The Ursatz: The Urlinie (descending melodic line from the 5th, 3rd, or 8th degree to the tonic) supported by the Bassbrechung (bass arpeggiation I–V–I). All tonal music is a prolongation and elaboration of this Ursatz. In C major: G–F–E–D–C (soprano) / C–G–C (bass).
narrative.n0desc: The complete musical surface: all chords, ornaments, melodies, passing notes, neighbour notes, arpeggios. This is the music as performed.
narrative.n1desc: Reduction eliminating surface ornaments. Only the main harmonic functions and prolongations remain.
narrative.n2desc: Reduction showing only tonal regions and major harmonic prolongations.
narrative.n3desc: The fundamental structure (Ursatz): Urlinie + Bassbrechung. The harmonic framework of the entire work in a few notes.
narrative.o0desc: Connects two structural notes by stepwise motion: C–D–E, the D disappears at the reduction level
narrative.o1desc: Leaves a structural note, moves one step away, returns: C–D–C, the D is a neighbour note
narrative.o2desc: Unprepared foreign note on a strong beat, resolved by descending stepwise motion
narrative.o3desc: Resolution of a note is delayed — the non-structural note holds on the strong beat and resolves afterwards
narrative.schenkerWarn: Classic mistake: Confusing Schenkerian reduction with simplification. Reduction does not remove notes "at random" — it identifies structural notes according to precise criteria (metric position, duration, harmonic role). Ornamental notes are not less important: they are the expressive surface. Reduction reveals structure; it does not judge value.
narrative.motiviqueP: Motivic analysis identifies the melodic cells of 2 to 6 notes that form the raw material of a work, then tracks their transformations. This level of analysis reveals the organic unity of a composition — how everything derives from very little.
narrative.beethInfoBox: Beethoven — 5th Symphony: The opening motif (G–G–G–Eb) is not just the beginning of the first movement. This rhythmic (3 eighth notes + quarter note) and melodic (descending minor third) motif organises all 4 movements — in the 2nd movement theme, in the 3rd movement fanfares, in the 4th movement finale transition. This is the very definition of organic unity.
narrative.beethDesc: Two simultaneous dimensions: rhythm (short–short–short–long) and melody (descending minor third). Both are inseparable in analysis. In the 2nd movement the rhythm is transformed, but the melodic third persists.
narrative.t0desc: The motif transposed to another degree — intervals are preserved
narrative.t0ex: Beethoven 5th: motif repeated from bar 2 a tone lower
narrative.t1desc: Intervals reversed — what went up now goes down and vice versa
narrative.t1ex: Bach: inversions of the subject in fugues
narrative.t2desc: The motif in doubled values — slower, more solemn
narrative.t2ex: In a higher register to mark the augmentation
narrative.t3desc: Only the first 2–3 notes of the motif — creates unease and urgency
narrative.t3ex: Beethoven 5th: development fragments the 4 notes into 2 + 2
narrative.s1desc: 2 to 6 notes appearing at the start and repeating. A distinctive interval or rhythm is the key.
narrative.s2desc: Mark every appearance of the motif in the score — transpositions and transformations included.
narrative.s3desc: Inversion, augmentation, diminution, fragmentation, sequence. How does the motif develop?
narrative.s4desc: Does the motif create exposition, development, climax? What narrative role does it play?
narrative.brahmsInfoBox: Brahms and motivic unity: In his quartets and symphonies, Brahms often derives the entire work from a single interval (a second or a third). This generating interval appears in the melody, the bass, the transitions, the counter-melodies — always recognisable, always transformed. Brahms's motivic analysis reveals a thematic density comparable to Bach's.
narrative.motiviqueWarn: Common mistake: Analysing each surface chord without seeking the deep structure. Saying "C–G–F–C" says nothing about structural significance. Motivic analysis complements harmonic analysis: on one side the vertical structure (chords), on the other the horizontal structure (motif and its destiny). Together they reveal compositional logic.
